desc: Rocky marine habitat along the narrows â€˘ Both Pelagic and Double-crested Cormorants, as well as Long-tailed Ducks in the winter

Erlands Point Preserve is a 30-acre county park located just downstream of Chico Salmon Park, which is typically the primary salmon viewing site for Salmon Tours.   Erlands Point Preserve contains the main stem of Chico Creek, several side channels that serve as important salmon rearing habitat, and a large man-made pond that are all hydrologically connected.  It also contains a large forested area with walking trails.
accessibility: Parking: Pull-out off Earlands Point Road NW | Wheelchair Access: No | Restrooms: None | Terrain: 0.8 mi path | Dogs Allowed: No

sitename: Poulsbo Waterfront to Oyster Plant Park

desc: Meandering along its namesake creek in Kitsap County, from Dyes Inlet in the south to Schold Farm in the north, Clear Creek Trail offers visitors a unique blend of urban accessibility and natural wonder. This family-friendly trail system winds its way through a diverse landscape of riparian areas, wetlands, and riparian forests. Built with accessibility in mind, most of the trail consists of hard-packed gravel surfaces and thoughtfully constructed boardwalks spanning wetlands and bridges crossing the creek. The Clear Creek Trail is a testament to community cooperation, built on both public and private land to preserve natural spaces within our urban environment.  Clear Creek Trail offers something for everyone, whether you're a casual walker, an eager bird watcher, or a cycling enthusiast. Come explore its winding paths and discover the natural beauty of Kitsap County.
accessibility: Parking: Paved lot at Silverdale Rotary Gateway Park | Wheelchair Access: Yes | Restrooms:  Pit toilet at Silverdale Dog Park | Terrain: 2.1 miles are hikeable via wheelchair starting from Silverdale Rotary Gateway Park with 73 foot elevation gain and minimal cross-slope. The trail consists of boardwalk, packed dirt and paved sections. About 50 feet of the trail has 2-3 inch stones jutting up from surface making for a very rocky ride on freeway side of wetlands area.  | Dogs Allowed: Yes

desc: This quick hike leads to a lovely wide sandy beach at the mouth of a marshy wetland. The trail winds through Douglas-fir, cedar, hemlock and alder along a broad wetland meadow. Be on the lookout for shorebirds and waterfowl as you hike. At the beach you'll find a profusion of russet hued Pacific madrone adorning a sheer bluff. Look for shells and crabs along the shoreline and enjoy a broad view of the Olympic Mountains to the west.
accessibility: Parking: Limited parking on shoulder of Twin Spits Rd | Wheelchair Access: None | Restrooms:  Portable Restroom | Terrain: Flat dirt path through forest. 1 Mile roundtrip to beach. | Dogs Allowed: no

sitename: Klingel Bryan Bear Wetland Wildlife Preserve

desc: Hike, walk the dog, hunt for mushrooms, or grab your mountain bike and go! With 635 acres to roam and 29 miles of trail you are likely to have the forest to yourself. Be sure to wear your waterproof hiking boots as the trails can get a bit muddy. It is easy to lose yourself in the solitude of the dense second-growth forest as 29 miles of rustic trails meander through 635 acres, offering an occasional glimpse of a black-tailed deer or Douglas squirrel. If you are lucky you can also spot black bears, cougars, coyotes, raccoons, and beavers, as well as the many species of birds that call Banner Forest home.
accessibility: Parking: Gravel Lot | Wheelchair Access: No | Restrooms: Portable Restroom | Terrain: Flat dirt path with varying widths and conditions | Dogs Allowed: Yes, on leash

desc: Guillemot Cove Nature Reserve is one of the hidden gems of Kitsap County Parks, with stunning scenic vistas of the Olympic Mountains, beautiful hiking trails through mature forests, and a rustic old house carved out of a cedar stump. It has a large parking area along Stavis Bay Road south of Seabeck, and offers several trails. Visitors to Guillemot Cove Nature Reserve start their adventure by hiking through groves of mature bigleaf maples and tunnels of huckleberry down to a wetland where Boycee Creek meets the Hood Canal. Beyond the marsh you will find a secluded beach with some of the best views of the Olympics, and especially The Brothers. This diverse mix of habitats (mature forests, riparian, wetland, and shoreline) are home to over 70 species of birds, including eagles, a variety of warblers, and seabirds like pigeon guillemots, loons, and black scoters. Visitors to Guillemot Cove Nature Reserve can delight in the natural soundscape of birdsong, rustling leaves, and lapping waves.
accessibility: Parking: Large parking area along Stavis Bay Road south of Seabeck | Wheelchair Access: None | Restrooms:  None | Terrain: 2.3 mile roundtrip to Dickerson Falls with trail varying in width and condition | Dogs Allowed: No

desc: The 2,200 acre tree farm has become a popular destination for the public as a scenic spot to walk dogs, hike, run, mountain bike or go horseback riding. The Lebers Lane trailhead and parking lot are used by nearly 100,000 visitors a year. Many people walk the short two mile round-trip trail to Dickerson Falls; others challenge themselves by continuing to Zach’s Lookout, hiking the South Loop Trail, or creating their own route among the many forest roads. It is our hope that our small network of private trails and forest roads becomes an integral part of a more extensive Central Kitsap trail network. We envision a trail system that allows people to walk or ride from Otto Jarstad Park through Ueland Tree Farm all the way to Green Mountain State Forest or Anderson Landing Preserve on Hood Canal. We ask all visitors to be alert and responsible when visiting our working tree farm, one that provides rock, gravel, and timber-related products to the community. This includes obeying all signs, staying out of active mine sites and logging areas, and being extra careful around heavy equipment. If you see something suspicious on the property, please report it.
accessibility: Parking: Gravel lot at Leber | Wheelchair Access: None | Restrooms:  None | Terrain: 2.3 mile roundtrip to Dickerson Falls with trail varying in width and condition | Dogs Allowed: Yes on leash
